title: Dog Knee Clicking: Causes, Triage, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog's knee clicking? Learn about common causes like luxating patellas and why most cases are low urgency unless accompanied by pain.
content_html:
TL;DR: A clicking sound in a dog's hind leg is usually caused by a luxating patella or joint gas and is rarely an emergency unless accompanied by pain or limping. Capturing a video of the movement at home is one of the most effective ways to help your veterinarian reach a diagnosis.

Why is my dog's hind leg making a clicking or popping sound?

A clicking or popping sound in a dog's hind leg is most commonly associated with the knee joint (the stifle). This sound often occurs when the kneecap, or patella, slides out of its natural groove and then snaps back into place—a condition known as a luxating patella. It can also be caused by a tear in the cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) or simply gas bubbles moving within the joint fluid, similar to a human cracking their knuckles.

When is a clicking knee in a dog considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Generally low if your dog is acting normally, eating well, and putting weight on the limb.
  • Monitoring: If there are no signs of distress, you can monitor the situation and schedule a non-emergency appointment.
  • Immediate Action: Contact a clinic within 24 hours if the clicking is accompanied by sudden limping, holding the leg up (non-weight bearing), or signs of significant pain.

How can a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my dog's joint issue?

  • Capture the movement: A video of your dog walking while the clicking occurs is a vital tool for diagnosis.
  • Overcome adrenaline: Because dogs often hide symptoms like limping or clicking at the clinic due to adrenaline, home video shows their natural state.
  • Assess alignment: A clear photo of your dog standing from behind helps the vet check for muscle wasting or abnormal joint alignment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A clicking sound during locomotion or flexion and extension of the stifle joint may indicate a medial meniscal injury, often associated with cranial cruciate ligament tears. Palpation can reveal patellar displacement, indicative of patellar luxation which can range in severity from mild, infrequent luxation to permanent displacement. Radiography is recommended to evaluate for joint effusion, degenerative joint disease, and limb deformities associated with chronic injuries or patellar luxation.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Is a clicking knee always painful for dogs?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Many dogs with mild luxating patellas do not show signs of pain, though the mechanical rubbing can lead to arthritis over time.

faqs.question: Should I restrict my dog's exercise if I hear clicking?
faqs.answer: It is best to limit high-impact activities like jumping or sprinting until a veterinarian has evaluated the joint to prevent further irritation.

faqs.question: Can supplements help stop the clicking sound?
faqs.answer: Joint supplements like glucosamine and chondroitin support joint health and lubrication, but they generally cannot fix the structural issues that cause clicking.
